extra.message: SQL Injection in Authentication Query - User-controlled input from username parameter is directly interpolated into SQL query using f-string, allowing SQL injection attacks and authentication bypass
Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) - User-controlled mathexp parameter is directly concatenated into Jinja2 template string without sanitization, allowing arbitrary Python code execution
Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) - Template rendering with unsanitized user input allows Remote Code Execution
Missing authentication and authorization on all endpoints - no auth middleware or controls implemented
Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) - user input concatenated directly into Jinja2 template allowing arbitrary code execution
Debug mode enabled in Flask application, exposing interactive debugger console that allows arbitrary Python code execution
Local File Inclusion (LFI) / Path Traversal - User-controlled filename parameter is passed directly to open() without path validation, allowing arbitrary file reads
Local File Inclusion (LFI) - Filename parameter from user input is used directly in fetchfile() without validation, enabling path traversal attacks
Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) - User-controlled URL is passed to requests.get() without validation, allowing access to internal services and cloud metadata
Insecure HTTPS Configuration - SSL/TLS certificate verification is disabled (verify=False), exposing the application to Man-in-the-Middle attacks
Passwords stored in plaintext without hashing or salting
Host Header Injection leading to XSS - untrusted Host header directly injected into HTML anchor tag
Local File Inclusion (LFI) / Path Traversal - User-controlled filename passed directly to open() without validation
Hardcoded Credentials - Plaintext admin and user credentials embedded in source code
SSL/TLS certificate verification disabled - allows man-in-the-middle attacks

Missing Authentication on lfivuln endpoint

The lfivuln handler reads arbitrary files from the filesystem based on user input without any authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can invoke this endpoint.

Impact: An unauthenticated attacker can read arbitrary files from the server filesystem, including sensitive configuration files, credentials, and source code.

Fix: Add authentication checks to verify the caller's identity before processing the request.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via user-controlled URL

The `rfivuln` function takes a user-supplied 'imagelink' parameter and passes it to `fetchimage()` which makes an HTTP GET request to the attacker-controlled URL using `requests.get(url=name, ...)`. An attacker can provide internal URLs (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/`, `http://localhost:6379/`, `file:///etc/passwd`) to access internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, or internal network resources.

Impact: An attacker can scan internal networks, access cloud metadata services (AWS/GCP/Azure), interact with internal services (Redis, databases), and potentially exfiltrate sensitive data or pivot to internal systems.

Fix: Implement URL validation with an allowlist of permitted domains/schemes. Block requests to private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 169.254.x, 127.x, ::1). Use a dedicated egress proxy. Validate the resolved IP address after DNS resolution to prevent DNS rebinding attacks.

Host Header Injection leading to XSS

The `hhivuln` function reads the `Host` header from the HTTP request and embeds it directly into an HTML anchor tag without any sanitization: `<a href='{h}'>`. An attacker can manipulate the Host header to inject malicious content. For example, setting Host to `javascript:alert(1)` or `'><script>alert(1)</script>` would result in XSS when the response is rendered in a browser.

Impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into the response by manipulating the Host header, potentially leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or phishing attacks against users who view the response.

Fix: Validate the Host header against an allowlist of expected hostnames. HTML-encode any user-controlled values before embedding them in HTML output. Do not use the Host header directly in HTML responses.

Reflected XSS / content injection via remote file content in RFI response

The content fetched from a user-controlled remote URL is directly embedded into the response without any sanitization or encoding. An attacker can point the `imagelink` parameter to a URL serving malicious HTML/JavaScript, which will be included verbatim in the response.

Impact: An attacker can specify a URL hosting malicious HTML/JavaScript content, which gets fetched by the server and reflected in the response. When a client renders this response, the malicious content executes, leading to XSS.

Fix: HTML-encode all fetched content before including it in responses. Validate the URL against an allowlist of trusted domains. Set strict Content-Type headers on responses.
Host Header Injection with HTML Injection/XSS - Host header is extracted from request and embedded directly into HTML anchor tag without sanitization, allowing XSS and cache poisoning attacks
Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences - Unsanitized Host header is embedded in HTML response, enabling header injection and XSS attacks
Reflected XSS via JSON body parameter - user input directly reflected in response without sanitization
SQL Error Information Disclosure - Raw database exception messages returned to user
Plaintext Password Storage - User passwords stored in database without hashing or encryption
Missing HTTP security headers (X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, HSTS)
Insecure SQLite threading configuration - check_same_thread=False disables thread safety, causing race conditions
Database file unconditionally deleted on startup - causes data loss and potential DoS
Improper exception handling with bare except clause - silently swallows errors and masks security events
No input size limits or rate limiting - allows memory exhaustion and brute force attacks

Reflected XSS / content injection via file content in LFI response

The content of a user-specified file is read and directly embedded into the response without any sanitization or encoding. If the file contains HTML or JavaScript content, it will be included verbatim in the response, potentially leading to XSS if rendered by a client.

Impact: An attacker can read files containing HTML/JavaScript content (or craft files with malicious content) and have that content reflected in the response, leading to XSS when rendered by a client.

Fix: HTML-encode file contents before including them in responses. Validate and sanitize the filename parameter. Restrict file access to a specific directory.

Reflected XSS via SSTI rendered template output

User-controlled input is used to construct a Jinja2 template string which is then rendered and returned in the response. Beyond the SSTI vulnerability, the rendered output can contain arbitrary HTML/JavaScript that is returned without sanitization, leading to XSS.

Impact: An attacker can inject HTML/JavaScript via the `mathexp` parameter which gets rendered by the Jinja2 template engine and returned in the response, leading to XSS when the response is rendered by a client.

Fix: Never construct Jinja2 templates from user input. Use parameterized template rendering with proper auto-escaping enabled. Sanitize all user input before including in responses.
Missing SECRET_KEY configuration in Flask application
Application bound to 0.0.0.0 (all network interfaces)
Insecure SQLite threading configuration - check_same_thread=False disables thread safety, causing race conditions
Improper exception handling with bare except clause - silently swallows errors and masks security events
OpenAPI specification exposes vulnerability details - self-documents attack surface for attackers
